items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.q: What does the cosmological argument try to prove?
items.correct: That the existence of the universe requires a first cause or necessary being — usually identified with God — to explain why anything exists at all.
items.misconception: The cosmological argument proves who created life on Earth.
items.random_wrong: The cosmological argument proves the Big Bang happened.
items.why: Students mix up cosmology (science) with the cosmological argument (philosophy). The argument is about why anything exists at all, not the specific origins of life or the Big Bang.

items.q: What is distinctive about the ontological argument?
items.correct: It tries to prove God's existence purely from the concept of God — a priori reasoning needing no empirical evidence about the world.
items.misconception: The ontological argument uses scientific evidence to prove God exists.
items.random_wrong: The ontological argument is the same as the cosmological argument.
items.why: Anselm's argument is famously a priori — pure reasoning from definition. Students try to fit it into evidence-based frameworks, missing what makes it uniquely controversial in philosophy.

items.q: Is the ontological argument an argument from experience?
items.correct: No — it is a priori: it starts from the concept of God as the greatest conceivable being and argues that such a being must exist in reality, using reason alone.
items.misconception: The ontological argument reasons from our experience of order in the world to a designer God.
items.random_wrong: The ontological argument is based on the testimony of mystics.
items.why: Arguments from order are teleological; from contingency, cosmological; from definition, ontological. Filing each argument in the right family is the first step to comparing their strengths.

items.q: Does the cosmological argument's premise apply to God himself?
items.correct: No — the premise concerns contingent, caused, or moved things; God is concluded to be a necessary, uncaused being, so 'what caused God?' misses how the argument is framed.
items.misconception: The cosmological argument claims everything without exception has a cause, so God needs one too.
items.random_wrong: The cosmological argument claims God is caused by the universe.
items.why: Whether a 'necessary being' is coherent (Hume's worry) is the serious objection; the playground retort attacks a premise the argument never uses.

items.q: Whose work contains the Five Ways?
items.why: It's Thomas Aquinas. “William Paley” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Paley's watchmaker analogy is an example of which type of argument?
items.why: Correct answer: Teleological. “Ontological” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Anselm's Proslogion written?
items.why: The real figure is 1078. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
